author: @edkoetsier339
text: Hi Rob. Axial Flux is a very efficient type of generator. Perhaps better when made for DC. In that configuration, it can't generate useful current, but I suppose it might be useful if you need a small amount of AC to trigger or measure something. There are easier ways to transform DC to AC, however.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree mate but this isn't axial flux - it's switched flux - one thing in axial flux the magnets are rotated over the coils in switched flux both the coils and the magnets are stationary

author: @grazianoturbogas
text: You could put all the magnets on one side, Or at least interpose an iron bar between them to close the magnetic circuit. All that air between the magnets in not ok for me. I suggest you to try. Anyway that could became a Flinn's motor.

replies.author: @mcbusinessmonkey
replies.text: You'll be attached to circuit if it's too close. A little more space and you'll be inducting magnetically. That's your flux. You won't get it if the magnets are too close.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes you could close the magnetic circuit but clearly you don't have to

author: @NorthernKitty
text:
The other day, there was a bright flash from my microwave followed by darkness just as the microwave finished heating my dinner.  At first, I was all excited, thinking my 15-year-old microwave had finally given out and I'd have an excuse to scavenge the transformer and other parts.

You can imagine how greatly disappointed I was to discover... nope, it was just the interior light bulb. 😋  
I'll just have to keep working it harder - it has to give out SOMETIME, darn it!!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - the first time I have heard of someone being happy when something breaks lol

author: @ronniepirtlejr2606
text:
Hey Robert, I built a Transformer from a recycled microwave Transformer,  watching one of your videos. As I turn up my variable Transformer,  the amperage jumps from 1.8 amp draw to 8 amps at 100 volts. I have a feeling that the insulation is breaking down at 100 volts. What is your idea on the that being a possibility?
It works fine below 100 volts!

replies.author: @1973shaner
replies.text: Oversaturation.

replies.author: @markallison1141
replies.text:
Is that 8 amps at 100 volts on the primary?
What kind of voltage and currnt are you getting out of the secondary?
As the power you produce out of the secondary increases the power in must also increase. For example if you are building a welder using the transformer the current in will jump way up when you are producing an arc even though the output voltage will be low.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's unlikely I think mate that insulation is rated at 2,000V isn't it? - but to be honest i don't really know what your set up so it is difficult to tell - I see a few folks are trying to help - would you mind giving a bit of a better description of your set up? - which coil are you using as your primary, what is your secondary and what Is it you are trying to run?

author: @pulesjet
text: I've built Built 30A BATTERY CHARGERS,  Spot welders and other PSU's using MOT Cores.   NEVER Chuck them out.
